name: Oxidative phosphorylation (electron transport chain)
summary: Mitochondrial pathway in which electrons from NADH and FADH2 flow through four respiratory complexes to O2, pumping protons whose electrochemical gradient drives ATP synthase to make the bulk of cellular ATP.
description: Oxidative phosphorylation (OXPHOS) couples the exergonic oxidation of NADH and FADH2 to the synthesis of ATP through Peter Mitchell's chemiosmotic mechanism. Four membrane complexes of the electron-transport chain (ETC) hand electrons down a redox gradient to molecular oxygen: Complex I (NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase) and Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase) both reduce the mobile lipid carrier ubiquinone; Complex III (cytochrome bc1) transfers electrons from ubiquinol to the mobile protein carrier cytochrome c via the protonmotive Q cycle; and Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase) reduces O2 to water. Complexes I, III and IV pump protons from the matrix (N-side) into the intermembrane space (P-side); Complex II does not. The resulting proton-motive force (a membrane potential of ~150–180 mV plus a pH gradient) drives Complex V (F1Fo-ATP synthase), a rotary motor that phosphorylates ADP, translocating ~4 H+ per ATP (including the cost of exchanging matrix ATP for cytosolic ADP through the adenine-nucleotide translocase and importing Pi through the phosphate carrier). Complete oxidation of one NADH yields roughly 2.5 ATP and one FADH2/succinate roughly 1.5 ATP (P/O ratios). Flux is set largely by the availability of ADP (respiratory or acceptor control) and is further tuned by allosteric ATP inhibition of cytochrome c oxidase, by matrix Ca2+, by the reversible binding of nitric oxide at Complex IV, and by IF1, which blocks wasteful ATP hydrolysis when the membrane potential collapses. The chain is the target of classic site-specific inhibitors — rotenone and piericidin (Complex I), malonate (Complex II), antimycin A (Complex III), cyanide, azide and carbon monoxide (Complex IV) and oligomycin (ATP synthase) — and can be uncoupled from ATP synthesis by protonophores or by uncoupling proteins (UCP1/thermogenin in brown adipose tissue) to generate heat. Incomplete reduction of O2 at Complexes I and III is the major source of mitochondrial superoxide.

sources.id: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8e, Ch. 19 (Oxidative Phosphorylation)

references.title: Lehninger Principles of Biochemistry, 8th ed. (Ch. 19, Oxidative Phosphorylation)
Mitchell P. Coupling of phosphorylation to electron and hydrogen transfer by a chemi-osmotic type of mechanism. Nature 191:144-148.
Sazanov LA. A giant molecular proton pump: structure and mechanism of respiratory complex I. Nature Reviews Molecular Cell Biology 16:375-388.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial matrix
compartments.description: N-side of the inner membrane; site of the TCA cycle, matrix NADH generation, and the F1 catalytic head of ATP synthase.

compartments.name: Inner mitochondrial membrane
compartments.description: Houses respiratory Complexes I–IV, ATP synthase, and the adenine-nucleotide and phosphate carriers; kept impermeable to protons so the proton-motive force can be maintained.

compartments.name: Mitochondrial intermembrane space
compartments.description: P-side of the inner membrane; pumped protons accumulate here (continuous with the cytosol via outer-membrane porins), building the proton-motive force.

metabolites.name: NADH
metabolites.description: Reduced electron donor oxidized by Complex I; carries a hydride (2 electrons).

metabolites.name: Ubiquinone (Coenzyme Q, oxidized)
metabolites.description: Lipid-soluble mobile electron carrier of the inner membrane; accepts electrons from Complexes I and II. Formula/charge omitted because the isoprenoid tail length (n, usually 10 in humans) is variable.

metabolites.name: Ubiquinol (Coenzyme QH2, reduced)
metabolites.description: Two-electron-reduced form of ubiquinone; the substrate oxidized by Complex III.

metabolites.name: Ferricytochrome c (oxidized, Fe3+)
metabolites.description: Oxidized state of the mobile intermembrane-space carrier cytochrome c (protein CYCS); accepts one electron at Complex III.

metabolites.name: Ferrocytochrome c (reduced, Fe2+)
metabolites.description: Reduced state of cytochrome c; donates one electron to Complex IV.

metabolites.name: Succinate
metabolites.description: TCA-cycle intermediate oxidized to fumarate by Complex II (succinate dehydrogenase), the entry point for FADH2-derived electrons.

metabolites.name: Dioxygen (O2)
metabolites.description: Terminal electron acceptor of the chain, reduced to water by Complex IV.

metabolites.name: Proton (H+)
metabolites.description: Translocated across the inner membrane by Complexes I, III and IV to build the proton-motive force, then returned through ATP synthase.

metabolites.name: Calcium ion (Ca2+)
metabolites.description: Matrix Ca2+ stimulates OXPHOS, matching ATP output to workload.

metabolites.name: Nitric oxide (NO)
metabolites.description: Endogenous signaling gas that reversibly inhibits cytochrome c oxidase in competition with O2.

enzymes.name: NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase (Complex I)
enzymes.description: Largest respiratory complex; pumps 4 H+ per NADH. UniProt P49821 is the core NADH-binding flavoprotein subunit NDUFV1 (holds the FMN and NADH site); the full complex has 7 mtDNA-encoded (ND1–ND6, ND4L) plus ~38 nuclear subunits.

enzymes.name: Succinate:ubiquinone oxidoreductase / succinate dehydrogenase (Complex II)
enzymes.description: The only respiratory complex that does NOT pump protons and the only membrane-bound TCA-cycle enzyme. UniProt P31040 is the covalent-FAD flavoprotein subunit SDHA carrying the succinate-oxidation site.

enzymes.name: Ubiquinol:cytochrome c oxidoreductase / cytochrome bc1 (Complex III)
enzymes.description: Runs the protonmotive Q cycle. UniProt P00156 is the mitochondrially encoded cytochrome b (MT-CYB), the catalytic core that carries the two b-type hemes and the Qo and Qi quinone-binding sites.

enzymes.name: Cytochrome c oxidase (Complex IV)
enzymes.oligomerization: 13-subunit monomer (functions as a dimer in vivo)
enzymes.description: Terminal oxidase; reduces O2 to water and pumps protons. UniProt P00395 is subunit I (MT-CO1), which houses the low-spin heme a and the heme a3–CuB binuclear O2-reduction center.

enzymes.name: F1Fo-ATP synthase (Complex V)
enzymes.description: Rotary nanomotor that couples proton flow through the Fo c-ring to ATP synthesis on the F1 head. UniProt P06576 is the catalytic F1 beta-subunit (ATP5F1B) that binds ADP + Pi.

enzymes.name: ADP/ATP translocase 1 (adenine-nucleotide translocase)
enzymes.description: Electrogenic antiporter that exports matrix ATP(4-) in exchange for cytosolic ADP(3-); the exchange consumes part of the membrane potential and is essential for continuous ATP export.

enzymes.name: Mitochondrial phosphate carrier
enzymes.description: Imports inorganic phosphate into the matrix in symport with H+ (electroneutral, thermodynamically equivalent to Pi/OH- antiport), supplying the Pi consumed by ATP synthase.

enzymes.name: ATPase inhibitory factor 1 (IF1)
enzymes.description: Endogenous unidirectional inhibitor that binds the F1 catalytic interface and blocks ATP hydrolysis (not synthesis) when the membrane potential collapses, e.g. during ischemia.

reactions.name: Complex I — NADH:ubiquinone oxidoreductase (proton pump)
reactions.equation: NADH + H+ + ubiquinone + 4 H+[matrix] <=> NAD+ + ubiquinol + 4 H+[intermembrane space]

provenance.notes: Transfers 2 electrons from NADH to ubiquinone and translocates 4 H+ to the P-side (one net matrix proton is consumed to form ubiquinol). Runs in reverse (reverse electron transport) under high proton-motive force.
FADH2-generating TCA-cycle step; feeds electrons into the ubiquinone pool but pumps NO protons, which is why succinate/FADH2 yields fewer ATP than NADH.

reactions.name: Complex III — ubiquinol:cytochrome c oxidoreductase (Q cycle)
reactions.equation: ubiquinol + 2 ferricytochrome c + 2 H+[matrix] -> ubiquinone + 2 ferrocytochrome c + 4 H+[intermembrane space]

provenance.notes: The core redox reaction (KEGG R02161) is ubiquinol + 2 ferricytochrome c -> ubiquinone + 2 ferrocytochrome c + 2 H+; the Q cycle adds vectorial translocation so that per 2 electrons a net 4 H+ appear on the P-side and 2 H+ are taken from the matrix.

reactions.name: Complex IV — cytochrome c oxidase (terminal O2 reduction)
reactions.equation: 4 ferrocytochrome c + O2 + 8 H+[matrix] -> 4 ferricytochrome c + 2 H2O + 4 H+[intermembrane space]

provenance.notes: Irreversible four-electron reduction of O2 to water with a high flux-control coefficient; of the 8 matrix protons taken up, 4 form water (chemical/scalar) and 4 are pumped to the P-side.

reactions.name: Complex V — F1Fo-ATP synthase (chemiosmotic ATP synthesis)
reactions.equation: ADP + Pi + 4 H+[intermembrane space] <=> ATP + H2O + 4 H+[matrix]

provenance.notes: KEGG R00086 is written in the hydrolysis direction (ATP + H2O + 4 H+[in] = ADP + Pi + 4 H+[out]); physiologically the enzyme runs in reverse, synthesizing ATP as protons flow down the gradient. ~4 H+ are translocated per ATP delivered to the cytosol when ANT/phosphate-carrier costs are included.

reactions.name: Adenine-nucleotide translocase (ADP/ATP exchange)
reactions.equation: ADP[intermembrane space] + ATP[matrix] -> ADP[matrix] + ATP[intermembrane space]

provenance.notes: Electrogenic 1:1 antiport (ATP4- out, ADP3- in) that supplies matrix ADP and exports newly made ATP; the charge imbalance is driven by, and partly dissipates, the membrane potential.

reactions.name: Mitochondrial phosphate carrier (Pi/H+ symport)
reactions.equation: Pi[intermembrane space] + H+[intermembrane space] -> Pi[matrix] + H+[matrix]

provenance.notes: Electroneutral H+-coupled import of Pi (equivalent to Pi/OH- antiport) that consumes the pH component of the proton-motive force and supplies the phosphate for ATP synthesis.

regulations.mechanism: Respiratory (acceptor) control: ADP availability is the primary driver of oxidative-phosphorylation flux. Rising matrix ADP accelerates ATP synthase, which relaxes the proton-motive force and thereby speeds electron transport through the whole chain (transition from resting state 4 to active state 3).

At a high intramitochondrial ATP/ADP ratio, ATP binds allosteric sites on cytochrome c oxidase (via subunit IV), lowering its turnover and keeping the membrane potential from rising to damaging levels — a feedback brake on the terminal step.

sources.id: Kadenbach B. Intrinsic and extrinsic uncoupling of oxidative phosphorylation. Biochim Biophys Acta (allosteric ATP inhibition of cytochrome c oxidase).

regulations.mechanism: Nitric oxide binds the reduced heme a3–CuB binuclear center of cytochrome c oxidase and competes reversibly with O2, acutely and reversibly throttling respiration; this lets NO redirect O2 and modulate mitochondrial oxygen sensitivity.

sources.id: Brown GC. Nitric oxide and mitochondrial respiration (reversible competitive inhibition of cytochrome c oxidase by NO).

regulations.mechanism: IF1 binds across the alpha-beta catalytic interface of F1 and blocks ATP hydrolysis (but not synthesis) when the membrane potential collapses and matrix pH falls, preventing ATP synthase from running backwards and wastefully consuming ATP during ischemia.

Matrix Ca2+, raised in parallel with contractile/energetic workload, stimulates ATP synthase and cytochrome c oxidase and (upstream) activates the matrix dehydrogenases that supply NADH, coordinately up-regulating OXPHOS to match ATP demand.

sources.id: Glancy B, Balaban RS. Role of mitochondrial Ca2+ in the regulation of cellular energetics. Biochemistry.

relations.note: The TCA cycle is the principal source of the matrix NADH oxidized by Complex I; the two pathways are tightly coupled through the matrix NADH/NAD+ pool.
Complex II IS succinate dehydrogenase — the only membrane-embedded TCA-cycle enzyme — physically linking the citric-acid cycle to the respiratory chain through the succinate/fumarate couple.

Cytosolic NADH produced by glycolysis is delivered to the chain indirectly through the malate–aspartate and glycerol-3-phosphate shuttles, which regenerate cytosolic NAD+ and feed electrons to Complex I or the ubiquinone pool.
Mitochondrial beta-oxidation feeds FADH2-derived electrons into the ubiquinone pool via the electron-transfer flavoprotein (ETF) and ETF:ubiquinone oxidoreductase, converging on the same chain.
